title: Intro to alphaN
text: install latest release version CRAN use: can install development version alphaN GitHub :
code: install.packages("alphaN") # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("jespernwulff/alphaN")

title: Intro to alphaN
text: vignette provides introduction basic functionality alphaN. full details methodology, please refer Wulff & Taylor (2024).

title: Intro to alphaN
text: Using alphaN function, can get alpha level need use obtain desired level evidence testing regression coefficient regression model. example: planning run linear regression model 1000 observations. thus set n = 1000. default BF 1 meaning want avoid Lindley’s paradox, .e. just want null alternative least equally likely reject null. Therefore, obtain evidence least 1, set alpha 0.0086.
code: alpha <- alphaN(n = 1000, BF = 1) alpha #> [1] 0.008582267

what: Plotting the relationship between the Bayes factor and p-value
title: Intro to alphaN
text: alphaN function works mapping p-value Bayes factor. relationship can shown using JAB_plot. instance:  alpha level needed achieve Bayes factor 1 shown red triangle plot. Lines achieving Bayes factors 3 (moderate evidence) 10 (strong evidence) also shown default. evident lower alpha level needed achieve higher evidence.
code: JAB_plot(n = 1000, BF = 1)

what: Alpha as a decreasing function of N
title: Intro to alphaN
text: important point procedure alpha set function sample size. larger sample size, lower alpha needed significant result can interpreted evidence alternative. graph illustrates relationship previous example:
code: seqN <- seq(50, 1000, 1) plot(seqN, alphaN(seqN), type = "l",      xlab = "n", ylab = "Alpha")

title: Intro to alphaN
text: set alpha level function sample size, need choose prior carefully. alphaN allows user choose four sensible prior options based suggestions previous literature: Jeffreys’ approximate BF (method = "JAB"), minimal training sample (method = "min"), robust minimal training sample (method = "robust"), balanced Type-Type-II errors (method = "balanced"). method = "JAB" good choice users want conservative small effects, method = "min" MLE misspecified, method = "robust" MLE misspecified sample size small, method = "balanced" Type-II errors costly. instance, achieve evidence 3 1,000 observations ensure balanced error rates, run package contains convenience function alphaN_plot allows quick comparison alpha function sample size four different methods:
code: alphaN(1000, BF = 3, method = "balanced") #> [1] 0.024221 alphaN_plot(BF = 3)

what: Calibrating alpha to effect-size and moment Bayes factors
title: Intro to alphaN
text: four methods invert Jeffreys’ approximate Bayes factor, whose prior places mode null effect. Klauer, Meyer-Grant & Kellen (2025) propose two alternative families Bayes factors whose priors instead center alternative hypothesis effect size ded_e chosen researcher: effect-size Bayes factors, moment Bayes factors, effects near zero priori implausible. alphaN can calibrate alpha level Bayes factors, answers question: alpha need significant result corresponds Bayes factor least BF alternative centered effect size actually care ? moment prior rules effects near zero, alpha level implies falls much faster sample size JAB: prior settings follow recommendations Klauer et al. (2025) can adjusted nu r arguments; see ?alphaN details, including recover calibration default (Jeffreys-Zellner-Siow type) Bayes factor Rouder et al. (2009).
code: # alpha needed at n = 1000 for moderate evidence (BF = 3), targeting a # medium-sized effect (de = 0.5) alphaN(1000, BF = 3, method = "ES", de = 0.5) #> [1] 0.002189564 alphaN(1000, BF = 3, method = "moment", de = 0.5) #> [1] 0.0004913521 ns <- c(100, 1000, 10000) round(rbind(JAB    = alphaN(ns, BF = 3),             ES     = alphaN(ns, BF = 3, method = "ES"),             moment = alphaN(ns, BF = 3, method = "moment")), 5) #>           [,1]    [,2]    [,3] #> JAB    0.00910 0.00255 0.00073 #> ES     0.01185 0.00219 0.00058 #> moment 0.00788 0.00049 0.00002

what: Bayes factors, joint tests, and clustered data
title: Intro to alphaN
text: Bayes factors behind method = "ES" method = "moment" available directly klauerBF(), reported test statistic can converted evidence prior set alpha level: joint test several coefficients, alphaN() klauerBF() accept q (number coefficients tested) p (number retained parameters, including intercept). uses exact regression-case Bayes factors Klauer et al. (2025), targeted effect size Cohen’s f scale: Klauer et al. (2025) derive Bayes factors normal linear model (t tests, linear regression, ANOVA). generalized linear models apply asymptotic sense four prior-fraction methods, prior-fraction methods natural choice , say, logit model modest sample. Note conceptual kinship across methods: upper argument balanced method targeted effect size de ES moment methods declare effect sizes consider realistic. balanced method treats uniform band Type Type II error rates balanced; ES moment priors concentrate focal effect size instead. can name plausible range, method = "balanced" matching upper expresses ; can name effect size actually care , method = "ES" "moment" de sharper statement. Finally, clustered (panel) data, Wulff & Taylor (2024) recommend calibrating total number observations, conservative choice, checking sensitivity effective sample size, computed ratio classical cluster-robust standard error:
code: klauerBF(n = 80, t = 2.24, de = 0.5) #> [1] 1.567758 # Alpha for the joint F test of q = 2 coefficients at n = 200, targeting a # medium effect alphaN(200, BF = 3, method = "ES", q = 2, p = 2, de = sqrt(0.15)) #> [1] 0.008427417 # Cluster-robust SE twice the classical one: n_e is four times smaller n_effective(n = 237, se = 0.1, se_robust = 0.2) #> [1] 59.25

title: Authors and Citation
text: Jesper Wulff. Author, maintainer. Luke Taylor. Author.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Wulff, J. N., & Taylor, L. (2024). alpha depend sample size: Bayesian-frequentist compromise significance testing. Strategic Organization. doi:10.1177/14761270231214429 Klauer, K. C., Meyer-Grant, C. G., & Kellen, D. (2025). Bayes factors hypothesis tests. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 32, 1070-1094. doi:10.3758/s13423-024-02612-2
code: @Article{,   title = {How and why alpha should depend on sample size: A Bayesian-frequentist compromise for significance testing},   author = {Jesper Wulff and Luke Taylor},   journal = {Strategic Organization},   year = {2024},   doi = {10.1177/14761270231214429}, } @Article{,   title = {On Bayes factors for hypothesis tests},   author = {Karl Christoph Klauer and Constantin G. Meyer-Grant and David Kellen},   journal = {Psychonomic Bulletin & Review},   year = {2025},   volume = {32},   pages = {1070-1094},   doi = {10.3758/s13423-024-02612-2}, }

what: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: goal alphaN help user set significance level function sample size. function alphaN allows users set significance level function sample size based evidence prior features desire. function JABt JABp converts test statistics pp-values sample size dependent Bayes factors. JAB_plot plots Bayes factor function pp-value, alphaN_plot plots alpha level function sample size given Bayes factor. Calculations based Wulff & Taylor (2024). enjoy package, please consider citing paper (see citation("alphaN")). version 0.2.0, alphaN() can also calibrate alpha level effect-size moment Bayes factors Klauer, Meyer-Grant & Kellen (2025), center alternative hypothesis effect size choosing (method = "ES" method = "moment"). ’re R user, may also interested associated Shiny app.

title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: install latest release version CRAN use: can install development version alphaN GitHub :
code: install.packages("alphaN") # install.packages("devtools") devtools::install_github("jespernwulff/alphaN")

title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: example: planning run linear regression model 1000 observations. thus set n = 1000. default BF 1 meaning want avoid Lindley’s paradox, .e., just want null alternative least equally likely reject null. Therefore, obtain evidence least 1, set alpha 0.0086.
code: library(alphaN)  alpha <- alphaN(n = 1000, BF = 1) alpha #> [1] 0.008582267

what: Targeting stronger evidence, and choosing the prior
title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: Raising BF asks evidence allowed reject, lowers alpha. method argument selects prior behind Jeffreys’ approximate Bayes factor:
code: # Moderate (BF = 3) and strong (BF = 10) evidence at n = 1000 alphaN(n = 1000, BF = 3) #> [1] 0.002549145 alphaN(n = 1000, BF = 10) #> [1] 0.0006911392  # Balancing Type I and Type II error rates instead of the default prior alphaN(n = 1000, BF = 3, method = "balanced") #> [1] 0.024221

what: Calibrating alpha to effect-size and moment Bayes factors
title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: methods "ES" "moment" (new 0.2.0) answer question: alpha need significant result corresponds Bayes factor least BF alternative centered effect size de actually care ? moment prior treats effects near zero priori implausible, alpha implies falls much faster sample size JAB:
code: # Moderate evidence, targeting a medium-sized effect (Cohen's d = 0.5) alphaN(1000, BF = 3, method = "ES", de = 0.5) #> [1] 0.002189564 alphaN(1000, BF = 3, method = "moment", de = 0.5) #> [1] 0.0004913521 ns <- c(100, 1000, 10000) tab <- rbind(JAB    = alphaN(ns, BF = 3),              ES     = alphaN(ns, BF = 3, method = "ES"),              moment = alphaN(ns, BF = 3, method = "moment")) colnames(tab) <- paste0("n = ", ns) round(tab, 5) #>        n = 100 n = 1000 n = 10000 #> JAB    0.00910  0.00255   0.00073 #> ES     0.01185  0.00219   0.00058 #> moment 0.00788  0.00049   0.00002

what: Turning regression output into Bayes factors
title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: JAB() computes Jeffreys’ approximate Bayes factor coefficient directly fitted lm() glm() object; JABt() JABp() t-statistic p-value (e.g., published paper):
code: set.seed(1) d <- data.frame(x = rnorm(200), z = rnorm(200)) d$y <- 0.2 * d$x + rnorm(200) m <- lm(y ~ x + z, data = d)  JAB(m, covariate = "x") #> [1] 22.50664  # From summary statistics alone JABt(n = 200, t = 2.8) #> [1] 3.56385 JABp(n = 200, p = 0.005) #> [1] 3.634824

what: Klauer Bayes factors, joint tests, and clustered data
title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: klauerBF() returns effect-size moment Bayes factor , reported statistic can converted evidence prior set alpha. joint F tests, alphaN() klauerBF() take q (coefficients tested) p (retained parameters) use exact regression-case Bayes factors Klauer et al. (2025). n_effective() implements Wulff & Taylor (2024) effective-sample-size check clustered data:

title: Set Alpha Based on Sample Size Using Bayes Factors
text: alphaN_plot() compares alpha function sample size across selection calibration methods (four prior fractions default):  JAB_plot() shows Bayes factor maps onto p-value given sample size, marking alpha levels needed evidence thresholds 1, 3, 10:
code: alphaN_plot(BF = 3,             methods = c("JAB", "min", "robust", "balanced", "ES", "moment")) JAB_plot(n = 1000, BF = 3)

what: Transforms t-statistics from a glm or lm object into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factors — JAB
title: Transforms t-statistics from a glm or lm object into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factors — JAB
text: Extracts test statistic one coefficient, every coefficient, fitted model object converts Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor, given sample size used fit.

title: Transforms t-statistics from a glm or lm object into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factors — JAB
code: JAB(glm_obj, covariate = NULL, method = "JAB", upper = 1)

title: Transforms t-statistics from a glm or lm object into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factors — JAB
text: glm_obj glm lm object. covariate name covariate want BF , string. default, NULL, returns named vector Bayes factor every coefficient except intercept (request intercept explicitly covariate = "(Intercept)" need ). method Used choice 'b'. Currently one : "JAB": choice b produces Jeffreys' approximate BF (Wagenmakers, 2022) "min": uses minimal training sample prior (Gu et al., 2018) "robust": robust version "min" prevents small b (O'Hagan, 1995) "balanced": choice b balances type type II errors (Gu et al., 2016) upper upper limit range realistic effect sizes. relevant method="balanced". Defaults 1 range realistic effect sizes uniformly distributed 0 1, U(0,1).

title: Transforms t-statistics from a glm or lm object into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factors — JAB
text: numeric value BF favour H1, named vector BFs covariate = NULL.

title: Transforms t-statistics from a glm or lm object into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factors — JAB
code: # Simulate data  ## Sample size n <- 200  ## Regressors Z1 <- runif(n, -1, 1) Z2 <- runif(n, -1, 1) Z3 <- runif(n, -1, 1) Z4 <- runif(n, -1, 1) X <- runif(n, -1, 1)  ## Error term U <- rnorm(n, 0, 0.5)  ## Outcome Y <- X/sqrt(n) + U  # Run a GLM LM <- glm(Y ~ X + Z1 + Z2 + Z3 + Z4)  # Compute JAB for "X" based on the regression results JAB(LM, "X") #> [1] 0.07981323  # Compute JAB for every coefficient at once JAB(LM) #>          X         Z1         Z2         Z3         Z4  #> 0.07981323 0.09386151 0.20528203 0.09049906 0.07918742   # Compute JAB using the minimum prior JAB(LM, "X", method = "min") #> [1] 0.1128729

what: Plots JAB as a function of the p-value — JAB_plot
title: Plots JAB as a function of the p-value — JAB_plot
text: Plots JAB function p-value

title: Plots JAB as a function of the p-value — JAB_plot
code: JAB_plot(n, BF = 1, method = "JAB", upper = 1)

title: Plots JAB as a function of the p-value — JAB_plot
text: n Sample size. positive numeric vector. BF Bayes factor like match. 1 avoid Lindley Paradox, 3 achieve moderate evidence 10 achieve strong evidence. method Used choice 'b'. Currently one : "JAB": choice b produces Jeffreys' approximate BF (Wagenmakers, 2022) "min": uses minimal training sample prior (Gu et al., 2018) "robust": robust version "min" prevents small b (O'Hagan, 1995) "balanced": choice b balances type type II errors (Gu et al., 2016) upper upper limit range realistic effect sizes. relevant method="balanced". Defaults 1 range realistic effect sizes uniformly distributed 0 1, U(0,1).

title: Plots JAB as a function of the p-value — JAB_plot
text: Prints plot.

title: Plots JAB as a function of the p-value — JAB_plot
code: # Plot JAB as function of the p-value for a sample size of 2000 JAB_plot(2000)

what: Transforms a p-value into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABp
title: Transforms a p-value into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABp
text: Converts two-sided p-value z- t-test Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor, given sample size.

title: Transforms a p-value into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABp
code: JABp(n, p, z = TRUE, df = NULL, method = "JAB", upper = 1)

title: Transforms a p-value into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABp
text: n Sample size. positive numeric vector. p two-sided p-value. z p-value based z- t-statistic? TRUE z. df z=FALSE, provide degrees freedom t-statistic. method Used choice 'b'. Currently one : "JAB": choice b produces Jeffreys' approximate BF (Wagenmakers, 2022) "min": uses minimal training sample prior (Gu et al., 2018) "robust": robust version "min" prevents small b (O'Hagan, 1995) "balanced": choice b balances type type II errors (Gu et al., 2016) upper upper limit range realistic effect sizes. relevant method="balanced". Defaults 1 range realistic effect sizes uniformly distributed 0 1, U(0,1).

title: Transforms a p-value into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABp
text: numeric value BF favour H1.

title: Transforms a p-value into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABp
code: # Transform a p-value of 0.007038863 from a z-test into JAB # using a sample size of 200. JABp(200, 0.007038863) #> [1] 2.670735  # Transform a p-value of 0.007038863 from a t-test with 190 # degrees of freedom into JAB using a sample size of 200. JABp(200, 0.007038863, z=FALSE, df=190) #> [1] 2.893791

what: Transforms a t-statistic into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABt
title: Transforms a t-statistic into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABt
text: Converts t-statistic (z-statistic) Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor, given sample size. Vectorized n t.

title: Transforms a t-statistic into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABt
code: JABt(n, t, method = "JAB", upper = 1)

title: Transforms a t-statistic into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABt
text: n Sample size. positive numeric vector. t t-statistic. method Used choice 'b'. Currently one : "JAB": choice b produces Jeffreys' approximate BF (Wagenmakers, 2022) "min": uses minimal training sample prior (Gu et al., 2018) "robust": robust version "min" prevents small b (O'Hagan, 1995) "balanced": choice b balances type type II errors (Gu et al., 2016) upper upper limit range realistic effect sizes. relevant method="balanced". Defaults 1 range realistic effect sizes uniformly distributed 0 1, U(0,1).

title: Transforms a t-statistic into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABt
text: numeric value BF favour H1.

title: Transforms a t-statistic into Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor — JABt
code: # Transform a t-statistic of 2.695 computed based on a sample size of 200 into JAB JABt(200, 2.695) #> [1] 2.670735

what: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
title: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
text: Computes alpha level required achieve desired level evidence, expressed Bayes factor, testing coefficient regression model. alpha level decreasing function sample size. Vectorized n BF.

title: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
code: alphaN(   n,   BF = 1,   method = "JAB",   upper = 1,   de = 0.5,   nu = NULL,   r = NULL,   q = 1,   p = 0 )

title: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
text: n Sample size. positive numeric vector. BF Bayes factor like match. 1 avoid Lindley's Paradox, 3 achieve moderate evidence 10 achieve strong evidence. method Bayes factor calibrate alpha . first four options invert Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor differ choice prior fraction 'b'; last two invert exact test-statistic Bayes factors Klauer et al. (2025), whose priors center alternative hypothesis prespecified effect size de. One : "JAB": choice b produces Jeffreys' approximate BF (Wagenmakers, 2022) "min": uses minimal training sample prior (Gu et al., 2018) "robust": robust version "min" prevents small b (O'Hagan, 1995) "balanced": choice b balances type type II errors (Gu et al., 2016) "ES": calibrates alpha effect-size Bayes factor (Klauer et al., 2025) "moment": calibrates alpha moment Bayes factor (Klauer et al., 2025), effects close zero priori implausible upper upper limit range realistic effect sizes. relevant method="balanced". Defaults 1 range realistic effect sizes uniformly distributed 0 1, U(0,1). Conceptually, upper plays "balanced" method role de plays "ES" "moment": declare effect sizes researcher deems realistic. upper treats uniform band whose Type Type II error rates balanced, whereas de singles focal effect size prior concentrates. de prespecified (targeted) effect size standardized units: Cohen's d q = 1 Cohen's f joint tests (scales coincide q = 1). used methods "ES" "moment". Defaults 0.5, medium effect; use 0.2 small 0.8 large effects (Cohen, 1988). nu Degrees freedom prior t distribution methods "ES" "moment". default, NULL, uses values recommended Klauer et al. (2025): 3 "ES" 5 + (q - 1) "moment". r Scale two prior mixture components method "ES". default, NULL, uses recommendation Klauer et al. (2025), r = sqrt((nu - 2)/(nu * q)) * de, requires nu > 2 de > 0; otherwise supply r explicitly. q Number coefficients tested jointly. used methods "ES" "moment". default, 1, test single coefficient; q > 1 alpha level set F test joint null q coefficients zero. p Number parameters retained model null, including intercept. used methods "ES" "moment". effective sample size Klauer et al. (2025) n - p; default, 0, reproduces one-sample form, treats sample size effective. regression coefficient small sample, setting p number estimated coefficients (including intercept) gives residual-degrees--freedom behavior exact regression case.

title: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
text: Numeric alpha level required achieve desired level evidence.

title: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
text: methods "ES" "moment" q = 1, alpha level found solving critical t value effect-size moment Bayes factor equals BF, converting critical value two-sided p-value t distribution n - p - 1 degrees freedom. q > 1, inversion runs noncentral-F form Bayes factors (Klauer et al., 2025, Table 4), alpha p-value F distribution (q, n - p - q) degrees freedom. implementation validated Bayes factors reported Tables 7 8 paper. moment prior assigns effects near zero priori density zero, alpha level implies decreases much faster n JAB. Klauer et al. (2025) derive Bayes factors normal linear model (t tests, linear regression, ANOVA). generalized linear models, "ES" "moment" calibrations hold asymptotic sense prior-fraction methods, whose Wald-based derivation covers generalized linear model directly; non-Gaussian models modest n, prefer prior-fraction methods. special case, setting method = "ES", nu = 1, de = 0 explicit scale (e.g. r = 1) calibrates alpha default (Jeffreys-Zellner-Siow type) Bayes factor Rouder et al. (2009). effective sample sizes greater 50,000, methods "ES" "moment" evaluate noncentral density ratio normal (chi-square) limit, accurate fraction percent .

title: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
text: Gu et al. (2016). Error probabilities default Bayesian hypothesis testing. Journal Mathematical Psychology, 72, 130–143.  Gu et al. (2018). Approximated adjusted fractional Bayes factors: general method testing informative hypotheses. British Journal Mathematical Statistical Psychology, 71(2).  Klauer, K. C., Meyer-Grant, C. G., & Kellen, D. (2025). Bayes factors hypothesis tests. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 32, 1070–1094. doi:10.3758/s13423-024-02612-2   O’Hagan, . (1995). Fractional Bayes Factors Model Comparison. Journal Royal Statistical Society. Series B (Methodological), 57(1), 99–138.  Rouder, J. N., Speckman, P. L., Sun, D., Morey, R. D., & Iverson, G. (2009). Bayesian t tests accepting rejecting null hypothesis. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 16, 225–237.  Wagenmakers, E.-J. (2022). Approximate objective Bayes factors p-values sample size: 3p(sqrt(n)) rule. PsyArXiv.  Wulff, J. N., & Taylor, L. (2024). alpha depend sample size: Bayesian-frequentist compromise significance testing. Strategic Organization, 22(3), 550–581. doi:10.1177/14761270231214429

title: Set the alpha level based on sample size for coefficients in a regression model — alphaN
code: # Plot of alpha level as a function of n seqN <- seq(50, 1000, 1) plot(seqN, alphaN(seqN), type = "l")   # Alpha calibrated to the effect-size Bayes factor (Klauer et al., 2025), # targeting moderate evidence for a medium-sized effect alphaN(1000, BF = 3, method = "ES", de = 0.5) #> [1] 0.002189564  # The same calibration under the moment Bayes factor alphaN(1000, BF = 3, method = "moment", de = 0.5) #> [1] 0.0004913521  # Alpha for the joint F test of q = 2 coefficients in a model with an # intercept and one further retained coefficient (p = 2), targeting a # medium effect on Cohen's f scale alphaN(200, BF = 3, method = "ES", q = 2, p = 2, de = sqrt(0.15)) #> [1] 0.008427417

what: Creates a plot of alpha as function of sample size for the chosen methods — alphaN_plot
title: Creates a plot of alpha as function of sample size for the chosen methods — alphaN_plot
text: Draws alpha decreasing function sample size selection calibration methods offered alphaN(). prior-fraction curves ("JAB", "min", "robust", "balanced") evaluated exactly every sample size; "ES" "moment" curves evaluated twelve log-spaced sample sizes interpolated spline log-log scale, keeps plot fast (expect roughly second computation per Klauer-type curve). Colors follow colorblind-safe Okabe-Ito palette.

title: Creates a plot of alpha as function of sample size for the chosen methods — alphaN_plot
code: alphaN_plot(   BF = 1,   max = 10000,   ylim = NULL,   methods = c("JAB", "min", "robust", "balanced"),   de = 0.5,   log = "" )

title: Creates a plot of alpha as function of sample size for the chosen methods — alphaN_plot
text: BF Bayes factor like match. 1 avoid Lindley's Paradox, 3 achieve moderate evidence 10 achieve strong evidence. max maximum number sample size. Defaults 10,000. ylim Limits y-axis. default, NULL, covers requested curves. Set e.g. c(0, 0.05) zoom small alpha levels. methods Character vector methods draw, subset c("JAB", "min", "robust", "balanced", "ES", "moment"). Defaults four prior-fraction methods, matching behavior earlier package versions. de prespecified (targeted) effect size standardized units: Cohen's d q = 1 Cohen's f joint tests (scales coincide q = 1). used methods "ES" "moment". Defaults 0.5, medium effect; use 0.2 small 0.8 large effects (Cohen, 1988). log Passed plot(): "" (default) linear axes, "x", "y", "xy" logarithmic ones. Logarithmic axes useful "moment" curve included, since falls much faster others.

title: Creates a plot of alpha as function of sample size for the chosen methods — alphaN_plot
text: Prints plot.

title: Creates a plot of alpha as function of sample size for the chosen methods — alphaN_plot
code: # Plot of alpha level as a function of n for a Bayes factor of 3 alphaN_plot(BF = 3)   # Compare JAB with the effect-size and moment calibrations alphaN_plot(BF = 3, methods = c("JAB", "ES", "moment"), log = "xy")

what: Write a settings report for a calibrated alpha level — alphaN_report
title: Write a settings report for a calibrated alpha level — alphaN_report
text: Generates short, human-readable Markdown report records every input behind calibrated alpha level together result: sample size, evidence target, calibration method prior settings, resulting alpha, decision rule. report designed attached preregistration protocol supplementary appendix, alpha level chosen data collection leaves citable trace. mirrors downloadable report package's companion Shiny application.

title: Write a settings report for a calibrated alpha level — alphaN_report
code: alphaN_report(   n,   BF = 1,   method = "JAB",   upper = 1,   de = 0.5,   nu = NULL,   r = NULL,   q = 1,   p = 0,   file = NULL,   width = 72 )

title: Write a settings report for a calibrated alpha level — alphaN_report
text: n Sample size. single positive number (one report describes one design). BF Target Bayes factor. single positive number. method Bayes factor calibrate alpha . first four options invert Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor differ choice prior fraction 'b'; last two invert exact test-statistic Bayes factors Klauer et al. (2025), whose priors center alternative hypothesis prespecified effect size de. One : "JAB": choice b produces Jeffreys' approximate BF (Wagenmakers, 2022) "min": uses minimal training sample prior (Gu et al., 2018) "robust": robust version "min" prevents small b (O'Hagan, 1995) "balanced": choice b balances type type II errors (Gu et al., 2016) "ES": calibrates alpha effect-size Bayes factor (Klauer et al., 2025) "moment": calibrates alpha moment Bayes factor (Klauer et al., 2025), effects close zero priori implausible upper upper limit range realistic effect sizes. relevant method="balanced". Defaults 1 range realistic effect sizes uniformly distributed 0 1, U(0,1). Conceptually, upper plays "balanced" method role de plays "ES" "moment": declare effect sizes researcher deems realistic. upper treats uniform band whose Type Type II error rates balanced, whereas de singles focal effect size prior concentrates. de prespecified (targeted) effect size standardized units: Cohen's d q = 1 Cohen's f joint tests (scales coincide q = 1). used methods "ES" "moment". Defaults 0.5, medium effect; use 0.2 small 0.8 large effects (Cohen, 1988). nu Degrees freedom prior t distribution methods "ES" "moment". default, NULL, uses values recommended Klauer et al. (2025): 3 "ES" 5 + (q - 1) "moment". r Scale two prior mixture components method "ES". default, NULL, uses recommendation Klauer et al. (2025), r = sqrt((nu - 2)/(nu * q)) * de, requires nu > 2 de > 0; otherwise supply r explicitly. q Number coefficients tested jointly. used methods "ES" "moment". default, 1, test single coefficient; q > 1 alpha level set F test joint null q coefficients zero. p Number parameters retained model null, including intercept. used methods "ES" "moment". effective sample size Klauer et al. (2025) n - p; default, 0, reproduces one-sample form, treats sample size effective. regression coefficient small sample, setting p number estimated coefficients (including intercept) gives residual-degrees--freedom behavior exact regression case. file Optional path. supplied, report also written file. width Maximum line width report; longer lines wrapped hanging indent. Defaults 72 characters.

title: Write a settings report for a calibrated alpha level — alphaN_report
text: report character vector lines, invisibly. report printed console.

title: Write a settings report for a calibrated alpha level — alphaN_report
code: alphaN_report(n = 1000, BF = 3, method = "JAB") #> # alphaN settings report #>  #> Generated on 2026-07-17 with alphaN 0.2.0.9000. #>  #> ## Inputs #>  #> - Sample size (n): 1,000 #> - Target Bayes factor: 3 (moderate evidence) #> - Calibration method: JAB: Jeffreys' approximate Bayes factor #>   (unit-information prior, b = 1/n) #>  #> ## Result #>  #> - Calibrated alpha level: 0.00255 #> - Decision rule: Reject H0 if the two-sided p-value of the coefficient #>   is at or below 0.00255. #> - Interpretation: a significant result then corresponds to a Bayes #>   factor of at least 3 in favor of the alternative under this prior. #>  #> ## Please cite #>  #> - Wulff, J. N., & Taylor, L. (2024). How and why alpha should depend on #>   sample size: A Bayesian-frequentist compromise for significance #>   testing. Strategic Organization, 22(3), 550-581. #>   doi:10.1177/14761270231214429  # Effect-size calibration, written to a file f <- tempfile(fileext = ".md") alphaN_report(n = 1000, BF = 3, method = "ES", de = 0.5, file = f) #> # alphaN settings report #>  #> Generated on 2026-07-17 with alphaN 0.2.0.9000. #>  #> ## Inputs #>  #> - Sample size (n): 1,000 #> - Target Bayes factor: 3 (moderate evidence) #> - Calibration method: ES: effect-size Bayes factor of Klauer, #>   Meyer-Grant & Kellen (2025) #> - Targeted effect size (de): 0.5 (Cohen's d) #> - Prior degrees of freedom (nu): 3 #> - Prior scale (r): 0.2887 #>  #> ## Result #>  #> - Calibrated alpha level: 0.00219 #> - Decision rule: Reject H0 if the two-sided p-value of the coefficient #>   is at or below 0.00219. #> - Interpretation: a significant result then corresponds to a Bayes #>   factor of at least 3 in favor of the alternative under this prior. #>  #> ## Please cite #>  #> - Wulff, J. N., & Taylor, L. (2024). How and why alpha should depend on #>   sample size: A Bayesian-frequentist compromise for significance #>   testing. Strategic Organization, 22(3), 550-581. #>   doi:10.1177/14761270231214429 #> - Klauer, K. C., Meyer-Grant, C. G., & Kellen, D. (2025). On Bayes #>   factors for hypothesis tests. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 32, #>   1070-1094. doi:10.3758/s13423-024-02612-2

what: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
title: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
text: Computes effect-size moment Bayes factor Klauer, Meyer-Grant, Kellen (2025) t statistic (one-sample test single regression coefficient) F statistic (joint test q coefficients). Bayes factors alphaN() inverts method = "ES" method = "moment", reported test statistic can converted evidence prior used set alpha level. Vectorized t (Fstat) n.

title: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
code: klauerBF(   n,   t = NULL,   Fstat = NULL,   q = 1,   p = 0,   method = "ES",   de = 0.5,   nu = NULL,   r = NULL )

title: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
text: n Sample size. positive numeric vector. t t-statistic. Used q = 1; supply either t Fstat (Fstat read squared t-statistic). Fstat F-statistic model comparison. Required q > 1. q Number coefficients tested jointly. default, 1, covers one-sample test test single regression coefficient. p Number parameters retained reduced model, including intercept. effective sample size Klauer et al. (2025) n - p; default, 0, one-sample case. test single coefficient regression model, p number estimated coefficients, including intercept. method "ES" effect-size Bayes factor "moment" moment Bayes factor. de prespecified (targeted) effect size: Cohen's d q = 1, Cohen's f joint tests (two scales coincide q = 1). Defaults 0.5. joint tests, Cohen (1988, Chapter 9) labels f^2 0.02, 0.15, 0.35 small, medium, large, de = sqrt(0.15) targets medium effect. nu Degrees freedom prior t distribution. default, NULL, uses recommendations Klauer et al. (2025): 3 "ES" 5 + (q - 1) "moment". r Scale prior mixture components method "ES". default, NULL, uses recommendation Klauer et al. (2025), r = sqrt((nu - 2)/(nu * q)) * de, requires nu > 2 de > 0; otherwise supply r explicitly.

title: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
text: numeric vector Bayes factor favour H1.

title: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
text: q = 1 Bayes factor evaluated noncentral-t form n - p - 1 degrees freedom, q > 1 noncentral-F form (q, n - p - q) degrees freedom (Table 4 Klauer et al., 2025). implementation validated printed Bayes factors Tables 7 8 paper. special case, q = 1, nu = 1, de = 0 explicit scale (e.g. r = 1) gives default (Jeffreys-Zellner-Siow type) Bayes factor Rouder et al. (2009).

title: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
text: Cohen, J. (1988). Statistical power analysis behavioral sciences (second edition). Lawrence Erlbaum.  Klauer, K. C., Meyer-Grant, C. G., & Kellen, D. (2025). Bayes factors hypothesis tests. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 32, 1070-1094. doi:10.3758/s13423-024-02612-2   Rouder, J. N., Speckman, P. L., Sun, D., Morey, R. D., & Iverson, G. (2009). Bayesian t tests accepting rejecting null hypothesis. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 16, 225-237.

title: Effect-size or moment Bayes factor from a t or F statistic — klauerBF
code: # Effect-size Bayes factor for t(79) = 2.24 targeting a medium effect # (Table 7 of Klauer et al., 2025) klauerBF(n = 80, t = 2.24, de = 0.5) #> [1] 1.567758  # The moment Bayes factor for the same statistic klauerBF(n = 80, t = 2.24, method = "moment", de = 0.5) #> [1] 0.9948127  # Joint test of q = 2 coefficients in a regression with 3 retained # parameters (Table 8 of Klauer et al., 2025, model M9) klauerBF(n = 175, Fstat = 1.17, q = 2, p = 3, de = sqrt(0.15)) #> [1] 0.06724721

what: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
title: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
text: Computes effective sample size recommended Wulff Taylor (2024) calibrating alpha clustered (panel) data, observations independent nominal sample size overstates information data: n_e = n * (se / se_robust)^2, total number observations deflated squared ratio classical cluster-robust standard error. Vectorized arguments (recycled).

title: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
code: n_effective(n, se, se_robust)

title: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
text: n Total number observations. positive numeric vector. se classical (non-robust) standard error coefficient. se_robust cluster-robust standard error coefficient.

title: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
text: numeric vector effective sample size.

title: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
text: Wulff Taylor (2024) recommend calibrating alpha total number observations, conservative choice, checking whether conclusions survive alpha Bayes factor recomputed effective sample size. cluster-robust standard errors typically exceed classical ones, n_effective() typically smaller n, yields larger calibrated alpha.

title: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
text: Wulff, J. N., & Taylor, L. (2024). alpha depend sample size: Bayesian-frequentist compromise significance testing. Strategic Organization, 22(3), 550-581. doi:10.1177/14761270231214429

title: Effective sample size from cluster-robust standard errors — n_effective
code: # A regression on 237 clustered observations where the cluster-robust # standard error is twice the classical one implies an effective sample # size four times smaller (Wulff & Taylor, 2024, Example 3) n_effective(n = 237, se = 0.1, se_robust = 0.2) #> [1] 59.25  # Alpha for moderate evidence at the nominal and the effective sample size alphaN(n = 237, BF = 3, method = "robust") #> [1] 0.02637516 alphaN(n = n_effective(237, 0.1, 0.2), BF = 3, method = "robust") #> [1] 0.0395262

title: alphaN (development version)
text: New function alphaN_report() writes preregistration-ready Markdown settings report: every input behind calibrated alpha (sample size, evidence target, method, prior settings), resulting alpha, decision rule, references cite. mirrors downloadable report companion Shiny application, can write straight file, hard-wraps lines (default 72 characters, see width) report reads well consoles, files, rendered documents. JAB() called without covariate now returns named vector Bayes factor every coefficient except intercept (remains available explicit request). alphaN_plot() JAB_plot() restyled: colorblind-safe Okabe-Ito palette, light grid lines, open axes, horizontal axis labels. alphaN_plot() additionally gains log argument (“x”, “y”, “xy”) helps fast-falling “moment” curve drawn next others, tick labels always use plain notation (“0.0001” “10,000”, never “1e-04”). New function klauerBF() exports effect-size moment Bayes factors Klauer, Meyer-Grant & Kellen (2025) alphaN() inverts: t statistic (one-sample test single regression coefficient) F statistic joint test q coefficients. alphaN() (methods "ES" "moment") gains arguments q p. q > 1 alpha level calibrated joint F test q coefficients exact regression-case Bayes factors Klauer et al. (2025, Table 4), implemented natively including Gaussian hypergeometric term; p sets number retained model parameters small-sample calibrations can use effective sample size n - p (residual degrees freedom). defaults (q = 1, p = 0) reproduce previous behavior exactly. moment-prior default nu now 5 + (q - 1) ES-prior scale recommendation generalizes r = sqrt((nu - 2)/(nu * q)) * de, following paper (unchanged q = 1). New function n_effective() computes effective sample size n * (se/se_robust)^2 Wulff & Taylor (2024) recommend sensitivity check calibrating alpha clustered (panel) data. alphaN_plot() gains methods argument can now draw "ES" "moment" curves alongside prior-fraction methods. regression-case implementation validated printed Bayes factors Table 8 Klauer et al. (2025), addition existing Table 7 anchors, q = 1 F form agrees validated t form near machine precision.

title: alphaN (development version)
text: Klauer, Meyer-Grant & Kellen now cited printed-issue details (2025, Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 32, 1070-1094) throughout; previously cited online-first year, 2024. ?alphaN states model scope "ES" "moment" methods: exact normal linear model, asymptotic (like prior-fraction methods) generalized linear models. citation("alphaN") now also lists Klauer et al. (2025) users "ES" "moment" methods.

title: alphaN 0.2.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-07-12

title: alphaN 0.2.0
text: alphaN() gains two methods based Klauer, Meyer-Grant & Kellen (2024, Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, doi:10.3758/s13423-024-02612-2): method = "ES" calibrates alpha effect-size Bayes factor, whose prior centers alternative hypothesis prespecified effect size, method = "moment" calibrates alpha moment Bayes factor, effects near zero priori implausible. New arguments de (targeted effect size, default 0.5), nu, r control priors, defaults following paper’s recommendations. moment prior rules near-zero effects, alpha level implies falls much faster n JAB. special case, method = "ES", nu = 1, de = 0 explicit r calibrates alpha default (Jeffreys-Zellner-Siow type) Bayes factor Rouder et al. (2009). implementation validated twelve Bayes factors printed Table 7 Klauer et al. (2024); checks part test suite.

title: alphaN 0.1.3
text: alphaN() JABt() now return correct results n vector method = "robust" method = "balanced". Previously, "robust" silently applied smallest sample size every element "balanced" failed unrelated error.

title: alphaN 0.1.3
text: functions now validate inputs fail informative error messages: mistyped method, missing df JABp(..., z = FALSE), p outside (0, 1], non-positive n BF, unknown covariate JAB() (now lists coefficients available model). JAB_plot() gained upper argument, passed underlying computations method = "balanced". alphaN_plot() gained ylim argument. default now covers four curves; previously y-axis fixed (0, 0.05), silently clipped "balanced" curve small Bayes factors. JAB() now determines sample size via nobs().

title: alphaN 0.1.3
text: ?JABp longer placeholder title. Corrected Wagenmakers (2022) reference (year, title) updated Wulff & Taylor reference published version (2024, Strategic Organization, doi:10.1177/14761270231214429) documentation, README, vignette. Added CITATION file companion paper. Fixed typos vignette documented JABp() expects two-sided p-value.

title: alphaN 0.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2025-07-13

title: alphaN 0.1.1
text: Removed vignette example depended unstable dataset.

title: alphaN 0.1.0
text: CRAN release: 2023-01-12 Added NEWS.md file track changes package. First CRAN submission.
